title: Directions & Travel

examples.kp: Magkayli ka king susunud a kanto.
examples.en: Turn left at the next corner.

examples.kp: Atyu ya ing bale king kayli.
examples.en: The house is on the left.

entries.notes: Directional noun for the left side. Used in the Wikivoyage Kapampangan phrasebook and multiple vocabulary guides. Phrase 'magkayli ka' means 'turn left'.

examples.kp: Magwanan ka king kanto.
examples.en: Turn right at the corner.

examples.kp: Atyu ya ing iskuwela king wanan ning dalan.
examples.en: The school is on the right side of the road.

entries.notes: Attested in the UCLA Kapampangan word list (1974) as entry 148. Cognate with Proto-Malayo-Polynesian *(ka-)wanan; related to Tagalog 'kanan'.

examples.kp: Siping ya ing tindahan keni.
examples.en: The store is near here.

examples.kp: Siping na tamu king balen.
examples.en: We are already near the town.

entries.notes: Attested in the UCLA Kapampangan phonetics word list (entry 102). Cognate with Proto-Philippine *sipiŋ meaning 'next to, in close proximity'. Also used in Tagalog with a slightly different shade (beside/next to).

examples.kp: Malawut ya ing Maynila king Kapampangan.
examples.en: Manila is far from Kapampangan territory.

examples.kp: Ali malawut ya ing tindahan; siping ya.
examples.en: The store is not far; it is near.

entries.notes: Confirmed in multiple Kapampangan vocabulary guides including The Kapampangan Traveller blog and common phrase lists. Distinct from Tagalog 'malayo'.

examples.kp: Atyu ya king babo ning lamesa.
examples.en: It is on top of the table.

examples.kp: Munta ka king babo; atyu ya karin ing kwartu.
examples.en: Go upstairs; the room is there.

entries.notes: Attested in UCLA Kapampangan word list (entry 40: 'upstairs'). Also confirmed in the Wikivoyage Kapampangan phrasebook and The Kapampangan Traveller vocabulary list.

examples.kp: Atyu ya king lalam ning lamesa.
examples.en: It is under the table.

examples.kp: Munta ka king lalam; keni ku iti milukluk.
examples.en: Go downstairs; I am seated here.

entries.notes: Attested in UCLA Kapampangan word list (entry 124: 'beneath'). Confirmed in the Wikivoyage phrasebook and multiple vocabulary sources. Also appears in Kapampangan idiomatic expressions.

examples.kp: Atyu ya ing kotsi king arap ning bale.
examples.en: The car is in front of the house.

examples.kp: Sumulung ka; datang ka king arap.
examples.en: Go straight; you will arrive at the front.

entries.notes: Confirmed in tagaloglang.com Kapampangan dictionary ('keng arap' = sa harap). Also appears in a traditional Kapampangan riddle as 'arap' (front). Related to Tagalog 'harap'.

examples.kp: Atyu ya ing tindahan king likuran ning bale.
examples.en: The store is behind the house.

examples.kp: Munta ka king likuran; karin ya ing puerta.
examples.en: Go to the back; the door is there.

entries.notes: Confirmed in Kapampangan riddle context: 'likuran' as back/behind. Related to Tagalog 'likuran/likod'. Synonym 'gulut' also used but less commonly documented.

examples.kp: Atyu ya king lub ning bale.
examples.en: He is inside the house.

examples.kp: Munta ka king lub ning kotsi.
examples.en: Get inside the car.

entries.notes: Attested in UCLA Kapampangan word list (entry 49: 'inside'). Extended form 'kilub' also used. Directional form 'papalub' means 'going inside/inward'. Cognate with Tagalog 'loob'.

examples.kp: Atyu ya king lual ning bale.
examples.en: He is outside the house.

examples.kp: Lumwal ka; mainit ya king lub.
examples.en: Go outside; it is hot inside.

entries.notes: Root of directional 'papaluwal' (going outside/outward) confirmed in vocabulary guides. The root 'lual' is the nominal form for outside/exterior. Related verb 'lumwal' means to go outside.

examples.kp: Buri ku arkilan ing kotsi.
examples.en: I want to rent a car.

examples.kp: Atyu ya ing kotsi king arap ning bale.
examples.en: The car is in front of the house.

entries.notes: Confirmed in the Wikivoyage Kapampangan phrasebook ('Buri keng arkilan ing kotsi' = I want to rent a car). From Spanish 'coche'. Variant 'kotse' also appears; 'kotsi' is the Kapampangan phonological form.

examples.kp: Sake kang dyip papunta king balen.
examples.en: Ride a jeepney going to town.

examples.kp: Pila ing pamasahe ning dyip?
examples.en: How much is the jeepney fare?

entries.notes: Used in Kapampangan context ('sake kung jeep' = I will ride a jeep) documented in vocabulary resources. Loanword from English via back-formation from 'jeepney'. The most iconic Philippine public transport vehicle.

examples.kp: Sake na ku king traysikel papunta king iskuwela.
examples.en: I will ride the tricycle going to school.

examples.kp: Ing traysikel malutu ya.
examples.en: The tricycle is red.

entries.notes: Cross-Philippine loanword from English 'tricycle' (Philippine motorized tricycle with sidecar). Widely used in Pampanga and attested in Kapampangan travel resources.

examples.kp: Tuknang ka keni, pakisuyo.
examples.en: Please stop here.

examples.kp: Tuknang naka ing kotsi king kayli.
examples.en: Stop the car on the left.

entries.notes: Confirmed in Wiktionary as Kapampangan: 'stop; cessation; break'. Also listed as synonym of 'patugut' in kaikki.org Kapampangan dictionary. Phrase 'Keni na mu, pakisuyo' (Please stop here) attested in philippinestravelmap.com.

examples.kp: Liku ka king wanan king susunud a dalan.
examples.en: Turn right at the next road.

examples.kp: Liku ka king kayli; siping na ing bale.
examples.en: Turn left; the house is nearby.

entries.notes: Root verb for turning/changing direction. Confirmed across Philippine languages as a shared Austronesian root. Wikivoyage Kapampangan phrasebook uses 'magkayli ka' and 'magwanan ka' for directional turns; 'liku' is the root.

examples.kp: Sumulung ka king dalan.
examples.en: Go straight along the road.

examples.kp: Sumulung ka; ali ka liku.
examples.en: Go straight ahead; do not turn.

entries.notes: Confirmed directly in the Wikivoyage Kapampangan phrasebook: 'sumulung ka' = 'straight ahead'. The imperative form 'sumulung ka' is the standard phrase for directing someone to go straight.

lessons.title: Left, Right & Straight
lessons.intro: Learn the three most essential direction words for navigating Pampanga: left, right, and straight ahead.

accepted: go straight ahead along the road
go straight ahead on the road
proceed forward ahead along the road
proceed forward ahead on the road
liku ka king wanan king kanto

lessons.title: Near & Far
lessons.intro: Describe how close or far something is. These two words are essential for asking about distances when traveling.

exercises.prompt: ____ ya ing balen; ali malawut. (The town is near; not far.)

lessons.title: Up, Down & Stop
lessons.intro: Learn words for vertical position and how to tell vehicles to stop — a must-know for riding jeepneys and tricycles.

exercises.prompt: Atyu ya king ____ ning lamesa. (It is on top of the table.)
Atyu ya king lalam ning lamesa.

lessons.title: Front & Back
lessons.intro: Describe where things are in relation to the front or back. Useful for giving and following directions around buildings and streets.

exercises.prompt: Atyu ya ing kotsi king arap ning bale.
accepted: the car is in front of the house
the car is at the front of the house
the car is in the front of the house
the car is at the the front of the house
the car is in ahead of the house
the car is at the ahead of the house
the car is in the ahead of the house
the car is at the the ahead of the house
the automobile is in front of the house
the automobile is at the front of the house
the automobile is in the front of the house
the automobile is at the the front of the house
the automobile is in ahead of the house
the automobile is at the ahead of the house
the automobile is in the ahead of the house
the automobile is at the the ahead of the house

exercises.prompt: Atyu ya ing tindahan king ____ ning bale. (The store is behind the house.)
Go to the back; the door is there.
accepted: munta ka king likuran; karin ya ing puerta

lessons.title: Inside & Outside
lessons.intro: Master the words for inside and outside — essential when boarding vehicles and describing locations.

exercises.prompt: Atyu ya king lub ning bale.
Atyu ya king ____ ning bale. (He is outside the house.)
Munta ka king lub ning kotsi.

lessons.title: Vehicles & Getting Around
lessons.intro: Learn the names of the vehicles you will use in Pampanga: the iconic jeepney, the tricycle, and the car.

exercises.prompt: Sake kang dyip papunta king balen.
accepted: ride a jeepney going to town
take a jeepney going to town
ride the jeepney going to town
ride a jeep going to town
take a jeep going to town
ride the jeep going to town

exercises.prompt: Sake na ku king ____ papunta king iskuwela. (I will ride the tricycle going to school.)

accepted: i want to rent a car
i would like to rent a car
i want to rent an automobile
i would like to rent an automobile

confidenceNotes: HIGH confidence (multiple independent sources): kayli (left), wanan (right), babo (above), lalam (below), lub (inside), kotsi (car), tuknang (stop), sumulung (straight ahead) — all confirmed in Wikivoyage phrasebook and/or UCLA 1974 word list and/or Wiktionary. MEDIUM-HIGH confidence: siping (near) — confirmed in UCLA word list as Kapampangan; malapit is the Tagalog cognate. malawut (far) — confirmed across multiple vocabulary guides. arap (front), likuran (back) — confirmed in Kapampangan riddle context and tagaloglang dictionary. lual (outside) — inferred from directional form 'papaluwal' attested in vocabulary guides; root 'lual' is the nominal form. liku (turn) — root attested across Austronesian languages; used in Kapampangan direction context. MEDIUM confidence: dyip (jeepney), traysikel (tricycle) — pan-Philippine loanwords widely used in Pampanga, attested in travel resources, though not in a dedicated Kapampangan dictionary entry.
